title: Depression and everyday life
citation: Kessler, R.C., Berglund, P., Demler, O., Jin, R., Koretz, D., Merikangas, K.R., Rush, A.J., Walters, E.E., & Wang, P.S. (2003). The epidemiology of major depressive disorder: Results from the National Comorbidity Survey Replication (NCS-R). JAMA, 289(23), 3095-3105.
content:
Depression touches every corner of daily life. Understanding how it does this can help make sense of experiences that might otherwise feel confusing, shameful, or inexplicable.

At work or in study, depression tends to impair concentration, slow thinking, and drain motivation. Tasks that were once routine require enormous effort. Deadlines are missed not because of laziness but because the cognitive and emotional resources needed to complete them are depleted. This often leads to a painful gap between what a person knows they are capable of and what they can currently manage, which can deepen feelings of worthlessness or self-criticism.

In relationships, depression creates distance. Withdrawal is one of its most consistent features. The energy required to socialise, respond to messages, or be present with people one cares about may simply not be available. Irritability, often underrecognised as a symptom of depression, can strain relationships further. The people around someone with depression may not understand what is happening and may interpret withdrawal as rejection or disinterest.

Physical health is also affected. Depression is associated with disrupted sleep, which compounds fatigue and cognitive difficulties. Appetite changes, either eating significantly less or turning to food for comfort, can affect physical wellbeing. Physical pain, including headaches and muscle aches, is more common in people with depression than is often acknowledged.

Perhaps most difficult is the effect of depression on the sense of self. Depression distorts self-perception, generating thoughts that the current state will never change, that one is a burden to others, or that past abilities and pleasures are gone permanently. These thoughts feel convincing but they are symptoms of the illness, not accurate assessments of reality.

Depression is an illness, not a personal failing. The difficulties it creates in everyday functioning are consequences of a treatable condition, not evidence of weakness or inadequacy.
